name: ⚡ The Anunnaki
connections: Abzu - fresh water abyss beneath earth

richMetadata.structure: The Anunnaki are organized hierarchically under Anu (sky god). They inhabit multiple levels: high Anunnaki (council in heaven), middle Anunnaki (executive functions), and lower Anunnaki (workers). They gather in Nippur and convene in divine assembly (Ekur) for cosmic decisions.
connections: Abzu - fresh water abyss beneath earth
richMetadata.significance: The Anunnaki represent divine bureaucracy and cosmic administration. They created humans to serve them and maintain cosmic order. Their existence illustrates Sumerian understanding of divine hierarchy, duty, and the interplay between celestial and terrestrial realms. The Anunnaki embody the principle of divine work and governance.
sources: Descent of Inanna - journey through realms
Cuneiform texts from Nippur and Sumeria

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Humanity
keyMyths.description: The Anunnaki, weary of their toil in the mines, tasked Enki and Ninhursag with creating humans from clay and the blood of a slain god to serve as their laborers. This marked the beginning of human existence and their role in serving the divine.

keyMyths.title: The Great Flood
keyMyths.description: Enlil, displeased with the noise and wickedness of humanity, decided to unleash a great flood to wipe them out. However, Enki, sympathetic to humankind, warned Utnapishtim (Ziusudra in other versions) and instructed him to build an ark, saving him and his family.

keyMyths.title: Enki and Ninhursag
keyMyths.description: This myth details the creation of various deities and beings, often with physical imperfections, from the union of Enki and Ninhursag. It highlights the creative power of these two deities and the consequences of their actions.

keyMyths.title: Inanna's Descent to the Underworld
keyMyths.description: Inanna (Ishtar), the Queen of Heaven, descends to the underworld ruled by her sister Ereshkigal. She faces trials and ultimately dies, only to be resurrected through the intervention of Enki, showcasing themes of death, rebirth, and divine power.

keyMyths.title: The Anunnaki's Division of the Cosmos
keyMyths.description: After the flood, the Anunnaki divided the cosmos amongst themselves, establishing their respective domains and responsibilities. This division solidified the hierarchical structure of the Sumerian pantheon and their influence over different aspects of the world.

sources.name: The Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature (ETCSL)
sources.description: A comprehensive online database containing translations and transcriptions of numerous Sumerian literary texts.
sources.title: The Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature (ETCSL)

sources.name: Black, J., Cunningham, G., Flückiger-Hawker, E., Robson, E., and Zólyomi, G., The Electronic Pennsylvania Sumerian Dictionary (EPSD)
sources.description: A detailed dictionary of the Sumerian language, providing definitions, etymologies, and textual examples.
sources.title: Black, J., Cunningham, G., Flückiger-Hawker, E., Robson, E., and Zólyomi, G., The Electronic Pennsylvania Sumerian Dictionary (EPSD)

sources.name: Dalley, Stephanie. Myths from Mesopotamia: Creation, The Flood, Gilgamesh, and Others.
sources.description: A collection of translated Mesopotamian myths, offering accessible versions of key narratives.
sources.title: Dalley, Stephanie. Myths from Mesopotamia: Creation, The Flood, Gilgamesh, and Others.

sources.name: Kramer, Samuel Noah. The Sumerians: Their History, Culture, and Character.
sources.description: A classic study of Sumerian civilization, providing historical and cultural context for understanding their mythology.
sources.title: Kramer, Samuel Noah. The Sumerians: Their History, Culture, and Character.

sources.name: Bottéro, Jean. Religion in Ancient Mesopotamia.
sources.description: An in-depth exploration of religious beliefs and practices in ancient Mesopotamia, including the role of the Anunnaki.
sources.title: Bottéro, Jean. Religion in Ancient Mesopotamia.
